schools.summary: Chrysostom: the priest is the common father, as it were, of all the world, and so it is proper that he should care for all, even as God whom he serves; from praying for all men two advantages result — first, hatred toward those who are without is done away, for no one can feel hatred toward those for whom he prays; Paul exhorting that, first of all, supplications, prayers, intercessions, and thanksgivings be made for all men — the breadth of public prayer reaching to all mankind (Paul, as a chief duty of the church's worship, urging the offering of prayer of every kind: 'supplications,' humble requests in need, 'prayers,' general devotions, 'intercessions,' pleadings on others' behalf, and 'giving of thanks,' gratitude, and that 'for all men,' the whole human family, of every sort and condition, so the church's heart, like God's, is to be enlarged in compassionate prayer for all).

themes: I exhort therefore, that, first of all
supplications, prayers, intercessions, and giving of thanks
be made for all men
the breadth of public prayer for all mankind

schools.summary: Chrysostom: if in order to put an end to public wars, tumults, and battles the priest is exhorted to offer prayers for kings and governors, much more ought private individuals to do it; for there are three very grievous kinds of war — the public, when our soldiers are attacked by foreign armies, the civil, when even in peace we are at war with one another, and the war within, when the individual is at war with himself; Paul charging that prayer be made for kings and all in authority, that the church may lead a quiet and peaceable life in all godliness and honesty — the prayer for rulers and its aim of a peaceful, godly life (Paul singling out from 'all men' the special object of prayer for 'kings and all that are in authority,' even heathen persecutors, and naming the end: 'that we may lead a quiet and peaceable life,' free from disturbance and persecution, 'in all godliness and honesty,' that under the shelter of public peace the church might serve God in piety and live in honest seriousness).

themes: for kings, and for all that are in authority
that we may lead a quiet and peaceable life
in all godliness and honesty
prayer for rulers, even heathen ones

schools.summary: Gill: not only to live peaceably and quietly under the government (since that is the ordination of God) and to live soberly, righteously, and godly (which his grace teaches), but to pray for all sorts of men, and for those set in the highest place of government, even though enemies and persecutors, is good in itself and in the sight of an omniscient God; Paul declaring that this (prayer for all men and for rulers) is good and acceptable in the sight of God our Saviour — the divine approval of wide, compassionate prayer (Paul confirming the duty by its pleasing God: such large-hearted intercession, even for enemies and persecutors, is 'good,' right and excellent in itself, and 'acceptable in the sight of God our Saviour,' well-pleasing to him who sees the heart, the very title 'God our Saviour' hinting why, for the God who saves and desires the salvation of men delights in the prayers of his people for all).

themes: for this is good and acceptable
in the sight of God our Saviour
the divine approval of wide prayer
well-pleasing to the God who saves

schools.summary: Gill: the salvation God wills that all men should enjoy is not a mere possibility of salvation, or putting them in a salvable state, or an offer, or a proposal of sufficient means in his word, but a real, certain, and actual salvation which he has determined they shall have, sure from his own appointment and the provision of Christ; and 'all men' he takes to mean all sorts and conditions of men, the elect of every kind (Jews and Gentiles, high and low), not every individual without exception; Paul declaring that God will have all men to be saved and to come to the knowledge of the truth — the much-debated will of God for the salvation of all (Paul grounding the duty of praying for all men in God's own gracious will toward all); the great debate: the Reformed reading 'all men' as all kinds and conditions of men, the elect out of every sort, whom God effectually wills and saves; others (the Arminian and general reading) taking it of every individual without exception, God sincerely desiring and willing the salvation of all though all are not saved; both agreeing the verse grounds wide prayer in God's saving goodness, which reaches to men of every sort.

debates.question: Whom does God 'will to be saved' — all kinds of men, or every individual?

sides.position: All sorts and conditions of men — the elect out of every kind, Jew and Gentile, high and low, whom God effectually wills and actually saves
Every individual without exception — God sincerely desires and wills the salvation of all, though all are not saved

themes: who will have all men to be saved
and to come unto the knowledge of the truth
the will of God for the salvation of all
grounding wide prayer in God's saving goodness

schools.summary: Gill: this does not so much regard the unity of God with respect to himself or his divine essence (though that is a truth) as the unity of God with respect to men — that there is but one God, the Creator of all men and, in a providential way, the Saviour of all men, of Jews and Gentiles alike, which is a reason why all sorts should be prayed for; Paul declaring that there is one God and one Mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus — the one God and one Mediator the ground of universal prayer and salvation (Paul grounding the breadth of prayer and of God's saving will in two great unities: 'one God,' the one Creator and Lord of all men of every nation, and 'one mediator between God and men,' that Mediator 'the man Christ Jesus,' truly man and, as elsewhere, truly God, the one appointed go-between who alone bridges the gulf between the holy God and sinful men, so that, since there is but one God and one Mediator for all, the prayer and the gospel are to reach to all).

themes: for there is one God
and one mediator between God and men
the man Christ Jesus
the one God and Mediator grounding universal prayer

schools.summary: Gill: what the Mediator gave as a ransom for men is 'himself' — his body and his soul, which were both made an offering for sin, his life which is the result of their union, his whole human nature as in union with his divine person, so might truly be said to be himself; this he gave into the hands of men, of justice, and of death, and that voluntarily, which shows his great love; Paul declaring that Christ gave himself a ransom for all, to be testified in due time — the ransom of Christ given for all, the substance of the gospel testimony (Paul naming the work of the one Mediator: he 'gave himself,' his whole self body and soul in his sacrifice, as 'a ransom,' a price of redemption paid to deliver captives, 'for all,' read with v.4 either as all kinds of men, the ransom certainly redeeming the elect of every sort, or as a ransom of universal sufficiency offered to all, and adding that this great fact was 'to be testified in due time,' published as the gospel's central witness in God's appointed season, so the redeeming death of Christ is the truth to be proclaimed to all).

debates.question: For whom did Christ give himself 'a ransom for all'?

sides.position: A ransom that actually redeems all kinds of men — the elect of every sort, certainly delivered
A ransom of universal sufficiency, given and offered for all men without exception

themes: who gave himself a ransom for all
to be testified in due time
Christ's whole self the price of redemption
the gospel's central witness

schools.summary: Gill: Paul was ordained or appointed to be a preacher of the gospel from all eternity, separated or set apart to it in time, and put into the ministry by Christ himself — not a common or ordinary preacher of the word but an apostle, an extraordinary officer in the gospel church; Paul declaring that he was ordained a preacher and apostle, and a teacher of the Gentiles in faith and verity, affirming with an oath that he speaks the truth — Paul's commission to proclaim the ransom to the Gentiles (Paul, having named the universal ransom 'to be testified,' pointing to his own office as the very channel of that testimony: 'ordained a preacher,' a herald of the good news, 'an apostle,' a sent one of Christ, and specifically 'a teacher of the Gentiles,' the great instance of God's saving will reaching beyond the Jews, teaching them 'in faith and verity,' the true faith and truth, and so weighty his claim that he seals it with a solemn asseveration, 'I speak the truth in Christ, and lie not').

themes: I am ordained a preacher, and an apostle
I speak the truth in Christ, and lie not
a teacher of the Gentiles in faith and verity
the channel of the universal testimony

schools.summary: Chrysostom: Christ said the hypocrites love to pray standing to be seen of men, and bade the believer enter his closet and shut the door; yet here Paul speaks of public prayer, requiring not the place only but inward purity, 'holy hands, without wrath and doubting'; Paul willing that men pray everywhere, lifting up holy hands, without wrath and doubting — the manner of acceptable prayer, purity, peace, and faith (Paul, returning to the duty of prayer, directing how and where it is offered: 'every where,' no longer confined as under the law to the temple but in all places, by 'men,' the public leading of prayer in the assembly belonging to them, and with the right disposition: 'lifting up holy hands,' the hands a symbol of a pure life, the worshipper clean from defiling sin, 'without wrath,' free from anger and malice toward others, and 'without doubting,' in the confidence of faith not wavering, so acceptable prayer requires a holy life, a loving heart, and a believing trust).

themes: I will therefore that men pray every where
lifting up holy hands
without wrath and doubting
acceptable prayer requiring purity, peace, and faith

schools.summary: Gill: in like manner as the men, the women are to join in public prayer (though not to lead or be the mouth of the church, which would be indecent), with purity of heart and hand, without murmuring and impatience toward God, without wrath and anger toward others, and in faith without doubting; and the apostle proceeds to direct their adorning; Paul directing that women adorn themselves in modest apparel, with shamefacedness and sobriety, not with elaborate hair, gold, pearls, or costly array — the modesty and sobriety that should mark Christian women's dress (Paul, turning from the men's prayer to the women, directing the manner of their adorning: clothed 'in modest apparel,' decent and becoming dress, and inwardly with 'shamefacedness,' a chaste modesty and reverence, and 'sobriety,' sound self-restraint, and not to glory in outward show, 'broided hair, or gold, or pearls, or costly array,' the extravagant ornament that betrays vanity, so the Christian woman's true ornament is inward and moral, not a gaudy display).

themes: that women adorn themselves in modest apparel
with shamefacedness and sobriety
not with broided hair, or gold, or pearls, or costly array
the true ornament inward and moral, not gaudy

schools.summary: Gill: by what becometh women professing godliness is meant not any particular grace only (was it, the fear of God might be designed, as the Syriac renders it), nor the whole of internal religion only, nor the form of godliness or scheme of gospel truth, nor only outward holiness of life, but the whole of all this, all religion internal and external; and their true ornament is good works; Paul declaring that women professing godliness should adorn themselves with good works — the inward and moral ornament fitting for godly women (Paul, having forbidden the gaudy outward ornament, naming the true adorning that 'becometh women professing godliness,' those who make profession of the fear and worship of God: not jewels and costly array but 'good works,' deeds of piety, charity, and holiness, which are the fitting and beautiful ornament of a Christian woman, an adorning that answers to her profession and pleases God).

themes: which becometh women professing godliness
with good works
the true adorning of godly women
good works fitting her profession

schools.summary: Chrysostom: great modesty and great propriety the blessed Paul requires of women, and that not only with respect to their dress and appearance but even in their speech, saying 'let the woman learn in silence' — that is, let her not speak at all in the church, a rule he also gave the Corinthians, 'it is a shame for women to speak in the church'; Paul charging that the woman learn in silence with all subjection — the quiet, submissive learning enjoined upon women in the assembly (Paul, regulating order in public worship, directing that 'the woman' is to 'learn,' a positive thing, taught and growing, but 'in silence,' quietly, not taking the public teaching role in the assembly, and 'with all subjection,' in due submission to the order God has set and to those who teach, so her place in the gathered church is that of a quiet, teachable learner, not a public instructor).

themes: let the woman learn in silence
with all subjection
quiet, submissive learning in the assembly
taught and growing, not a public instructor

schools.summary: Gill: women may teach in private, in their own houses and families — they are to be teachers of good things, to bring up their children in the nurture and admonition of the Lord, and the law or doctrine of a mother is not to be forsaken any more than a father's instruction (Timothy himself profited much from the private teaching of his mother Eunice and grandmother Lois) — but Paul forbids the public teaching of and authority over men; Paul declaring that he suffers not a woman to teach nor to usurp authority over the man, but to be in silence — the restriction of public teaching and authority, with its sphere defined (Paul, defining the woman's role in the assembly, 'suffers not,' does not permit, a woman publicly 'to teach' the men in the church 'nor to usurp authority over the man,' to exercise rule and dominion over him, but bids her 'be in silence' in that public sphere; not a forbidding of all teaching whatever, women rightly teaching children and other women and in their own households, but specifically the public teaching of and ruling over men in the church).

debates.question: What teaching by women does Paul forbid?

sides.position: Specifically the public teaching of and exercise of authority over men in the gathered church — not all teaching whatever, since women rightly teach children and other women and instruct in their own households

themes: I suffer not a woman to teach
nor to usurp authority over the man
but to be in silence
the public teaching of men restricted, household teaching affirmed

schools.summary: Gill: Adam was first formed immediately by God, out of the dust of the earth, and the breath of life was breathed into him and he became a living soul (so wonderfully and fearfully was he made); after this he was put into the garden of Eden to dress it, and all the creatures were brought to him to give them names, and still a help meet or companion was not found for him — all this while Eve was not as yet formed, but afterward, and out of him; Paul grounding the order of the sexes in the fact that Adam was first formed, then Eve — the priority of Adam in creation as the ground of order (Paul giving the first reason for the woman's place: the order of creation itself, 'for Adam was first formed, then Eve,' Adam made first and Eve afterward and, as elsewhere, out of him and for him, so the headship belongs to the man not by mere custom but by God's own ordering in the creation, a priority that grounds the submission Paul has enjoined).

themes: for Adam was first formed
then Eve
the priority of Adam in creation
headship grounded in God's creation order

schools.summary: Gill: there is no need to say with interpreters that Adam was not deceived first, or not immediately but by Eve, or that he is never said in Scripture to be deceived (as Melchizedek is never said to have father or mother); the apostle's positive assertion is to be taken without such limitations or qualifications — Adam was not deceived by the serpent, but the woman was; Paul observing that Adam was not deceived but the woman, being deceived, was in the transgression — the second reason drawn from Eve's being deceived in the fall (Paul giving a second ground for the order he enjoins, from the history of the fall: 'Adam was not deceived,' not the one the serpent beguiled, 'but the woman being deceived was in the transgression,' Eve being the one deceived by the serpent and first fell into the transgression; not to make the woman the worse sinner, for Adam sinned knowingly and bore the greater guilt as head, but to note that in the matter of being led astray the woman was deceived, a fact bearing on the order of teaching and rule).

themes: and Adam was not deceived
but the woman being deceived was in the transgression
the argument from Eve's deception in the fall
bearing on the order of teaching and rule

schools.summary: Gill: this is not meant of Eve (though she is doubtless saved, having a sense of her sin and shame for it, a revelation of the Messiah, and faith in him), but rather of any woman, particularly such as profess godliness, who shall be saved; Barnes: the promise is designed to alleviate the apparent severity of the preceding remarks; Paul declaring that she shall be saved in childbearing, if they continue in faith, charity, and holiness with sobriety — the comfort to women amid the curse, the much-debated 'saved in childbearing' (Paul, having spoken of the woman's part in the transgression, adding a word of comfort and hope); the debate on its meaning: whether the woman shall be brought safely through the perils of childbirth (a temporal preservation), or shall be saved eternally notwithstanding the sorrow of childbearing laid on her in the curse, or 'saved through THE childbearing,' the bringing forth of the Messiah the seed of the woman; and Paul adding the condition marking the truly saved, 'if they continue in faith and charity and holiness with sobriety,' so the woman's hope, like every believer's, is bound up with persevering faith and a holy life.

debates.question: What does 'she shall be saved in childbearing' mean?

sides.position: The godly woman shall be brought safely through the perils of childbirth — a temporal preservation
She shall be saved (eternally) notwithstanding the sorrow of childbearing laid on her in the curse
Saved through THE Childbearing — the bringing forth of the Messiah, the promised seed of the woman

themes: notwithstanding she shall be saved in childbearing
if they continue in faith and charity and holiness with sobriety
the much-debated comfort amid the curse
the woman's hope bound up with persevering faith
